questions.question: Explain the difference between argc and argv. What are their types and what do they represent?
keyPoints: argc is an integer representing the count of arguments
argv is an array of character pointers (char *argv[])
argv[0] contains the program name
argv[argc] is NULL (sentinel)
argc is always >= 1

questions.question: What is the purpose of the return value from main()? How can this value be accessed in a shell script?
keyPoints: Return value indicates exit status to OS
0 typically means success, non-zero means failure
Value is stored in $? shell variable
Only lower 8 bits are significant
Can also be accessed via wait() system call

questions.question: Describe two methods to access environment variables in a C program.
keyPoints: Method 1: Using third parameter envp in main()
Method 2: Using global extern char **environ
Both contain NAME=value format strings
Both terminated by NULL pointer
getenv() from stdlib.h is another common method

questions.question: What is the difference between exit() and return from main()?
keyPoints: exit() performs cleanup operations before termination
exit() flushes all open stdio streams
exit() calls functions registered with atexit()
return from main() returns to the caller directly
Both pass exit status to parent process

questions.question: Explain why void main() is not considered standard-compliant in C.
keyPoints: C standard defines main() as returning int
void main() is a non-standard extension
Different compilers may handle it differently
May cause undefined behavior
Always use int main() for portable code

questions.question: Write a C program that prints all command-line arguments in reverse order along with their indices.
keyPoints: Use argc to determine number of arguments
Iterate backwards from argc-1 to 1 (skip argv[0])
Print index and corresponding argument
Return 0 for successful completion
Consider edge case: no arguments besides program name

questions.question: Can main() be called recursively? Justify your answer with an example if possible.
keyPoints: Yes, main() is a regular function in C
Can take its address and call it recursively
Example: main(argc, argv) recursive call
Risk of stack overflow without proper base case
Generally considered bad programming practice

questions.question: Write a complete C program that accepts command-line arguments and environment variables, then demonstrates: (a) parsing all arguments, (b) displaying all environment variables, (c) returning appropriate exit status.
keyPoints: Use int main(int argc, char *argv[], char *envp[])
Loop through argv to display arguments
Loop through envp to display environment variables
Set exit status based on argument count or content
Include proper headers and return statement

questions.question: Explain the complete process of how command-line arguments are passed from the shell to the main() function in a Unix system. Include the role of the kernel, exec system call, and argv memory layout.
keyPoints: Shell parses command line arguments
Kernel creates user stack before program execution
argc pushed onto stack as integer
argv pointers pushed pointing to argument strings
argv array and strings laid out in memory
envp follows argv
Program's _start routine sets up registers and calls main()

questions.question: Design and implement a robust argument parser in C that handles: (i) positional arguments, (ii) optional flags (-v, -h), (iii) options with values (-o value), (iv) error handling for invalid arguments.
keyPoints: Define structure for parsed arguments
Iterate through argv starting from index 1
Check for flag format (- prefix)
Distinguish between flags and options with values
Set appropriate flags in parsed structure
Handle errors with helpful messages
Return non-zero exit status on error
